extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system comprising an insulated box with a heatbed, sensors, a controller, and a software system for communication and simulation. It operates in thermal control environments, integrating cyber-physical components to regulate temperature. Key characteristics include: (1) a heatbed with thermal inertia, (2) sensor-based data collection for temperature monitoring, (3) a controller implementing bang-bang control logic, (4) integration with simulation models for predictive analysis, and (5) real-time interaction with a Digital Twin via communication protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides the following services: (1) Real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard plotting of time-series data from controller and driver messages; (2) On-demand calibration using automated parameter estimation (Model B with four free parameters); (3) Anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation via Kalman filter correlation of measured data to unmeasured heatbed temperature; (4) What-if simulations through co-simulations using historical/real-time PT data; (5) Self-adaptation loop enabling dynamic reconfiguration of the controller in response to environmental changes (e.g., introducing cold objects). Target users include engineers and system operators for monitoring, optimization, and predictive control.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Key tools and technologies include: (1) RabbitMQ [4] as a communication server for message brokering between controller, low-level driver, and DT components; (2) Kalman filter for state estimation and anomaly detection; (3) Simulation models (two-parameter and four-parameter variants) for thermal inertia prediction; (4) Time-series database for storing and visualizing sensor data; (5) Open-source repository [6] hosting hardware/software specifications; (6) Low-level driver with safety protocols for PT interaction; (7) Bang-bang controller implementation for temperature regulation. Enablers include model calibration algorithms, co-simulation frameworks, and real-time data streaming protocols.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message broker (AMQP protocol), with Kalman filter processing of historical/real-time data for anomaly detection. Self-adaptation loop requires low-latency response to detect plant behavior changes and reconfigure controller parameters.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Single digital twin instance with centralized control architecture. The system includes multiple components (controller, low-level driver, communication server) but operates as a unified CPS-PT system without explicit decentralized or multi-instance coordination mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated integration of modeling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization technologies. Components include RabbitMQ for message passing between controller and low-level driver, Kalman filter for state estimation, co-simulation for what-if scenarios, and time-series databases for visualization. System-wide coordination involves parameter calibration and dynamic reconfiguration of the Kalman filter.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Data exchange via RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol) with external systems. Open-source hardware/software repository enables integration with third-party tools. Communication server acts as a bridge for data exchange between physical twin and digital twin components, supporting interoperability with enterprise systems through standardized messaging.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include simulation models (behavioral, analytical) with parameters such as C_air (J K⁻¹), G_box (J K⁻¹), C_heater (J K⁻¹), and G_heater (J K⁻¹). Data is collected from the Physical Twin (PT) via sensors over time, stored in time series databases, and used for real-time visualization and dashboard plotting. Calibration algorithms update models with parameters (e.g., Model A: C_air=616.56, G_box=0.65; Model B: C_air=486.12, G_box=0.856, C_heater=33.65, G_heater=0.87). Models are validated through co-simulations and Kalman filters for state estimation (e.g., heatbed temperature).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical components include a heatbed (thermal actuation) and a controller (cyber-physical interface). Actuation ranges are tied to heating parameters (e.g., C_heater=33.65 J K⁻¹). Control mechanisms involve scheduling experiments (e.g., cooling to safe temperatures, ramping heating) and re-configuring controllers via self-adaptation loops. Remote control interfaces are implied through software systems and calibration algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors measure temperature (unit: J K⁻¹) with spatial distribution across the incubator. Sampling frequencies and data transmission rates are not explicitly specified, but calibration experiments generate data trajectories. Accuracy and precision are inferred through model calibration (e.g., Model B's improved thermal inertia prediction). Data collection mechanisms include time series databases and Kalman filter correlation with measured data.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires parameter calibration (e.g., Model B's four parameters outperform Model A's two for thermal inertia prediction). Validation methods include co-simulations, Kalman filters, and comparison with historical/real-time PT data. Uncertainty sources include sensor accuracy, model parameter estimation, and environmental variability. Error detection uses Kalman filters for anomaly detection, and quality assurance involves iterative calibration and self-adaptation loops.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (e.g., DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital twin via RabbitMQ. Data types include temperature readings (-55°C to +125°C, 0.5°C accuracy). Communication protocol: RabbitMQ with MQTT-like message passing. Transmission frequency: periodic readings (low-level driver reads temperature data) and real-time updates. Event triggers: sensor data collection, system state changes. Data quality assurance: calibration algorithms update models with best parameter estimates. Preprocessing: temperature data uploaded to communication server. Integration mechanisms: RabbitMQ server relays data to models and monitors.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (fan/heatbed control) sent via RabbitMQ messages. Command types: actuator control signals. Message formats: structured messages through RabbitMQ. Validation: safe protocol in low-level driver ensures device safety. Feedback systems: Kalman filter correlates measured data to heatbed temperature. Conflict resolution: prioritizes safety protocols in low-level driver. Command execution confirmation: low-level driver confirms state updates to RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (message broker) for sensor-actuator and twin communication. Network topology: client-server architecture (RabbitMQ server on Raspberry Pi). Bandwidth/latency: lightweight RabbitMQ ensures low-latency messaging. Reliability: RabbitMQ's mature, fault-tolerant design. Security: safe protocol in low-level driver for device safety. Redundancy: no explicit redundancy mentioned, but RabbitMQ's deployment flexibility supports failover.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platforms: Raspberry Pi (edge) with optional cloud deployment. Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi handles control logic and data processing. Storage: time-series database for dashboard visualization. Scalability: open-source architecture supports modular expansion. Deployment models: edge (Raspberry Pi) and cloud. Availability: RabbitMQ's deployment flexibility ensures uptime. Reliability: RabbitMQ's lightweight design and safe protocol ensure system stability.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle includes development stages such as model calibration (section 3), deployment with real-time visualization (section 4), and ongoing adaptation via self-adaptation loops (section 4). Representation types transition from initial simulation models (two/four free parameters) to integrated systems with Kalman filters and co-simulations. Lifecycle governance involves continuous calibration, model reconfiguration, and interoperability through RabbitMQ [4] for data exchange between PT and DT.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The process uses methodologies like model calibration (section 3.3), co-simulation for what-if scenarios (section 4), and version control via open-source repositories [2]. Quality assurance includes validation of thermal inertia prediction with four-parameter models. Evolution strategies involve recalibrating Model B, updating Kalman filters (section 4), and adaptive control loops that reconfigure controllers based on experimental data. RabbitMQ [4] enables modular component integration.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes anomaly detection via Kalman filters [16], state estimation of heatbed temperature, and optimization of controller parameters through what-if simulations. Analytics processes correlate measurement data with models (section 4). User interaction occurs via dashboards for real-time visualization. Confidence measures are derived from Kalman filter residuals and simulation accuracy metrics.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with open-source standards (GitHub [2]), RabbitMQ [4] message broker protocols, and Industry 4.0 integration frameworks. Interoperability is achieved through standardized communication interfaces (RabbitMQ) and modular component design. Certification requirements are implied via open-source compliance and adherence to CPS modeling practices.

makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,

Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**

develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.
